calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Feb-22-2023 Vacated

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jun-02-2022 Set For Trial On Mar-06-23 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Apr-14-2022 Continued To Jun-15-22 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-25-2022 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS WAVE PLASTIC SURGERY CENTER, INC., WAVE PLASTIC SURGERY CENTER SAN FRANCISCO, INC. and PETER LEE'S DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

Defendant's demurrer to cause of action three for promissory fraud is overruled. (See CACI 1902 - False Promise.) The Second Amended Complaint ("SAC") pleads sufficient facts regarding a misrepresentation at paragraphs 19 and 39. Indeed, defendant's moving papers ignore the allegations at paragraph 19, which are incorporated by reference into the third claim, where Dr. Lee confirmed that he would use plaintiff's own rib bone for the procedure. The claim is pleaded with sufficient particularity and less specificity is required where "the facts lie more in the knowledge of the opposite party." (Tenet HealthSystem Desert, Inc. v. Blue Cross of Cal. (2016) 245 Cal.App.4th 821, 838.)

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling order in compliance with CRC3.1312.

Judge: Suzanne Bolanos, Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/SRB)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Feb-01-2022 Continued To Apr-27-22 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-21-2021 Continued To Feb-23-22 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-24-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS WAVE PLASTIC SURGERY CENTER, INC., WAVE PLASTIC SURGERY CENTER SAN FRANCISCO, INC., AND PETER LEE DEMURRER TO AMENDED COMPLAINT.

No appearances are made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's demurrer to cause of action 3 for "Fraud by Misrepresentation and/or Concealment" is sustained with leave to amend. Intentional misrepresentation, concealment, and false promise are three distinct causes of action. (See CACI 1900-Intentional Misrepresentation; CACI 1901 - Concealment; CACI 1902 - False Promise.) Here, plaintiff combines concealment and intentional misrepresentation claims into one cause of action, which is contrary to Cal. Rule of Court 2.112. In addition, plaintiff really appears to be attempting to plead a false promise claim. (See FAC, 19 [Dr. Lee confirmed that he would use plaintiff's own rib bone]; 13, 39 [representations regarding prices; plaintiff charged for use of own rib bone].) Plaintiff is granted 20 days leave to amend to clarify exactly what fraud claim/s he is pursuing. Plaintiff alleges sufficient facts regarding an intent to deceive. (See FAC 19.) That element is often demonstrated by circumstantial evidence and less specificity is required where "the facts lie more in the knowledge of the opposite party." (Tenet HealthSystem Desert, Inc. v. Blue Cross of Cal. (2016) 245 Cal.App.4th 821, 838.)

The demurrer to cause of action 4 for violation of the Patient Access Law, Health & Safety Code, Sec. 123100 et seq.. is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff reasonably discovered that defendant used a cadaver bone on or about March 20, 2020. (FAC, 21.) Approximately a year later, plaintiff requested his medical records and "his attorney" also requested them thereafter. (FAC, 22-23.) The only reasonable inference from these allegations is that plaintiff requested his records to aid in prospective litigation rather than to facilitate the provision of medical care. In Maher v. County of Alameda (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 1340, 1353-1354, the court explains that the Patient Access Law does not apply in such circumstances. "[I]n our view, the Patient Access Law was not intended to provide redress for patients whose attorneys are seeking access to medical records in contemplation of litigation against the health care provider." (Id. at 1354.) Moreover, Plaintiff concedes that after he filed the instant action, Defendants produced his medical records. (FAC, 23; Opp. at 6:6-7.)

The demurrer to cause of action 5 for violation of the UCL is overruled. Plaintiff must allege facts to support the claim "with reasonable particularity." (Khoury v. Maly's of California, Inc. (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 612, 618.) Here, plaintiff sufficiently alleges an unfair or fraudulent business practice claim based on defendant's false advertisements and representations regarding the use of plaintiff's rib bone for his medical procedure. (See Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 452 [liberally construe pleading].) Plaintiff cannot plead an unlawful business practice claim based on the Patient Access Law because as explained, it is inapposite.

The parties are reminded to comply with the meet and confer requirements of Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 430.41. Future filings attacking the pleadings (if any) that require a meet and confer will be taken off calendar if there is no compliance.

Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/EPS)

No appearances are made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's unopposed motion to strike the claim for punitive damages is granted. Plaintiff concedes that there has been no compliance with Code Civ. Proc. section 425.13 [order required to seek punitive damages against health care provider].

Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.
